⚠️ The Problem: The “First-In, First-Out” Trap
When developers first connect an autonomous agent to an event stream — like a security monitoring system — they typically use a standard FIFO (First-In, First-Out) queue. The agent simply processes tasks in the exact chronological order they arrive.
On a quiet Tuesday, this works fine. But during a coordinated cyberattack on a Monday morning, a Security Operations (SecOps) system might generate 10,000 automated alerts in under an hour.
If your agent is processing alerts chronologically, it might spend its first three hours diligently investigating 9,000 low-level phishing emails triggered by a marketing bot. Meanwhile, alert number 9,001 — a critical zero-day data exfiltration occurring on your main production database — is sitting silently at the back of the line, waiting for the agent to finish its busywork.
The result? The Chronological Catastrophe.

• The Noise Blindspot: High-volume, low-risk events completely drown out low-volume, existential threats.

• SLA Breaches: Critical issues that require a 5-minute response time are mathematically guaranteed to fail when buried under a mountain of mundane tasks.
